metadescription.value: Read about inspiring Rolex-sponsored adventures and how intrepid explorers have gone to the top of Mount Everest, the North Pole and the sea bottom wearing Rolex watches.

description.value: “Every high-flying, mountain-climbing, spelunking, sailing, trekking, deep-diving explorer has lived adventures and made discoveries that have helped shape the nature of civilization, one way or another. Their stories have become legends. Ah! If only their Rolexes could speak.” Sylvia Earle

text.value: “Every high-flying, mountain-climbing, spelunking, sailing, trekking, deep-diving explorer has lived adventures and made discoveries that have helped shape the nature of civilization, one way or another. Their stories have become legends. Ah! If only their Rolexes could speak.” Sylvia Earle.

legendRoller.value: The Deepsea Under The Pole expedition supported by Rolex was a two-month ski trek to the moving polar ice floe of the North Pole. The purpose of this expedition was documentation. With frequent stops to scuba dive, the team took unprecedented underwater images of the vanishing polar ice cap, along with hundreds of scientific observations related to human physiology and global climate change.

In 1960, Swiss oceanographer Jacques Piccard and Lieutenant Don Walsh, navigated the Trieste, on its nine-hour plunge into the deepest known depression on the Earth’s surface. The Rolex Deep Sea Special experimental watch that had been attached to the outside of the bathyscaphe resurfaced in perfect working order after having endured a pressure of more than one metric tonne per square centimetre.

James Cameron’s submersible DEEPSEA CHALLENGER was equipped with a hydraulic manipulator arm for gathering samples and with multiple cameras for capturing images of the landscape and life forms in some of the world’s deepest zones. Scientists across the country are still poring over images, caring for cultures, and examining deep-sea DNA as they try to learn more about how the biological and physical sciences look and operate in the ocean’s depths.

On 29 May 1953, Sir Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay, fuelled with extraordinary determination, were the first to reach Mount Everest’s 8,848-metre summit and achieved the goal that dozens of other earlier expeditions had attempted without success: to stand on the top of the world.

Spending as many waking hours as possible under water, photographer David Doubilet captured some of the most iconic images of our blue planet. Researchers and conservationists rely on his work to inspire public concern and focus the attention of policymakers on the urgency of protecting the oceans.

Sylvia Earle’s work has been at the frontier of deep ocean exploration for over four decades. She has led more than a hundred expeditions worldwide that have amounted to more than 7,000 hours underwater. In 1979, she walked untethered on the sea floor at a lower depth than any other woman had before. Later, in 1986, she tied the world solo dive depth record descending 1,000 metres in Deep Ocean Engineering’s submarine, equipped with technologies for scientific ocean research and exploration.

pageTitle.value: Joining forces for the future - Environment

pageCaption.value: For many years Rolex has supported individuals and organizations whose aims have been to explore the Earth, find innovative solutions for positive change and raise awareness of critical environmental issues.

WaPageTitle.value: Joining forces for the future - Environment

metadescription.value: Discover how Rolex joins forces with geographical and marine societies, explorers, conservationists, geologists and glaciologists to improve the future.

previewTitle.value: Joining forces for the future - Environment

pageCaption.value: Glacial microbiologist Joseph Cook says the top few metres of the Arctic’s ice are like a “frozen rainforest”. His research is a journey of discovery that reveals how ice micro-organisms on the Greenland ice sheet shape our world.

pageTitle.value: Gentle giant of the ocean - Rolex Awards

pageCaption.value: Kerstin Forsberg is protecting threatened giant manta rays by working with local communities to promote awareness and appreciation of these gentle giants and to assist fishermen find alternative income through ecotourism.
